title: Top 10 Horny Quarantine Erotic Thrillers | The Big Picture
format: Stuck inside, Sean and Amanda bring in Wesley Morris to rank the American erotic thriller: Joe Eszterhas, Adrian Lyne, Michael Douglas's ten-title résumé, and the AIDS-era paranoia underneath all that saxophone and Vaseline-lensed cinematography.

segments.heading: What makes an erotic thriller
segments.note: Sean, Amanda, and Wesley on the genre's rules, its Joe Eszterhas/Adrian Lyne lineage, and why it died out

segments.heading: The Top 10 Horny Quarantine Erotic Thrillers
segments.note: each host picked three, chronological order, Fatal Attraction was the one unanimous pick

picks.title: Body Heat
picks.note: Lawrence Kasdan's directing debut, and a Double Indemnity remake with all the sex that movie only implied. William Hurt and Kathleen Turner have believable, overheated chemistry; the plot, Amanda says, goes off a cliff the moment they get together, and that's fine.

picks.title: Body Double
picks.note: Brian De Palma's Hitchcock-fixated, extremely strange thriller about an actor drawn into watching a woman across the canyon. Melanie Griffith's contract-negotiation scene as porn star Holly Body is, per Wesley, one of the great unheralded moments of the genre.

picks.title: Jagged Edge
picks.note: Glenn Close as a defense attorney who sleeps with her murder-suspect client, played by a very young, very persuasive Jeff Bridges. Wesley's test case for the genre's core mechanic: someone has to pay for the sex.

picks.title: Fatal Attraction
picks.note: The one all three agreed had to be on the list without discussion. Michael Douglas and Glenn Close, previously covered on The Rewatchables, so they don't relitigate it here.

picks.title: Sea of Love
picks.note: Al Pacino and Ellen Barkin, written by Richard Price. The most New York entry on the list, and Sean's pick partly because it's really a movie about male friendship with a woman dropped into the middle of it.

picks.title: Basic Instinct
picks.note: Joe Eszterhas writing the genre's own rulebook and then blowing past it. Wesley calls it the high-water mark, and reads its ice-pick sexuality as a distorted echo of AIDS-era gay panic.

picks.title: Single White Female
picks.note: Barbet Schroeder's roommate-from-hell thriller. Amanda and Wesley agree it's thin on the actual erotic-thriller mechanics, but Jennifer Jason Leigh's performance carries it anyway.

picks.title: Body of Evidence
picks.note: Madonna on trial for using sex as a murder weapon, with Willem Dafoe. Wesley's pick for pure commitment to absurdity, coming right after Basic Instinct blew the genre's ceiling off.

picks.title: Wild Things
picks.note: Kevin Bacon, Neve Campbell, Denise Richards, and Matt Dillon in a movie Sean calls a self-aware postmortem on the genre: trashy for 45 minutes, then, per Roger Ebert's review that Sean quotes, revealed to be smarter than it looked.

picks.title: Unfaithful
picks.note: Adrian Lyne again, with Diane Lane and Richard Gere. Amanda's pick and the one she says affected her most as a teenager, for the train-flashback scene edited by Anne Coates.

segments.heading: The one that doesn't quite count
segments.note: Cher and Dennis Quaid in Suspect (1987)

films.title: Suspect
films.note: Wesley's litmus test in reverse: no sex actually happens between Cher's public defender and Dennis Quaid's juror, so by his own rule it isn't really an erotic thriller, even though it's the show's most-referenced film of the episode.

films.title: Deep Water
films.note: Adrian Lyne's return to directing after over a decade off, with Ben Affleck and Ana de Armas, scripted in part by Sam Levinson. One of Sean and Amanda's most anticipated films of the year, if it makes it to theaters.
